verses.text: But king Solomon loved many strange women, together with the daughter of Pharaoh, women of the Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, Zidonians, and Hittites; Of the nations concerning which the LORD said unto the children of Israel, Ye shall not go in to them, neither shall they come in unto you: for surely they will turn away your heart after their gods: Solomon clave unto these in love. And he had seven hundred wives, princesses, and three hundred concubines: and his wives turned away his heart. Guidance: The chapter pivots from peak to collapse. The opening 'But' (Hebrew vav-disjunctive) is the most ominous conjunction in 1 Kings. Everything that follows is the answer to the question: how did the wisest king in Israel's history end up the cause of the kingdom's division? The answer: foreign wives. The narrator lists five non-Israelite peoples whose princesses Solomon married: Pharaoh's daughter (already noted in chapter 3), Moabites, Ammonites, Edomites, Zidonians (Phoenicians), Hittites. The cross-reference: Deut 7:3-4 had explicitly forbidden marriage with these peoples 'for surely they will turn away your heart after their gods.' The chapter quotes the prohibition word-for-word; the narrator wants the reader to register that Solomon did not stumble accidentally — he violated a specific Mosaic command, the very command the LORD had highlighted as the gateway to apostasy. 'Solomon clave unto these in love.' Hebrew davaq — clung, joined to. The same verb used for Adam clinging to his wife (Gen 2:24) and for the proper Israelite clinging to the LORD (Deut 10:20). Solomon's clinging is misplaced; the loyalty owed to God and to a single covenant-wife is dispersed across hundreds of foreign women. The numbers: 700 wives 'princesses' (sarot — royal-rank, daughters of foreign rulers, brought into the household for political alliance) and 300 concubines (pilagshim — lower-status secondary wives). One thousand women total in his harem. The chapter is showing us the deuteronomic warning of Deut 17:17 fully materialized: 'Neither shall he multiply wives to himself, that his heart turn not away.' Solomon multiplied them past any ancient parallel. The verdict: 'his wives turned away his heart.' Hebrew vayyattu nashav et-libbo — the same verb (natah — to turn aside, incline) that Deut 17:17 had used in warning. Solomon's heart was turned. The chapter is teaching us that the moral architecture of compromise has a clear sequence: foreign-marriage entanglements → foreign-religion accommodation → divided heart → withdrawn divine favor. Solomon did not wake up one morning a pagan; he gradually allowed his thousand foreign attachments to displace the singular allegiance to the LORD.

For it came to pass, when Solomon was old, that his wives turned away his heart after other gods: and his heart was not perfect with the LORD his God, as was the heart of David his father. For Solomon went after Ashtoreth the goddess of the Zidonians, and after Milcom the abomination of the Ammonites. And Solomon did evil in the sight of the LORD, and went not fully after the LORD, as did David his father. Then did Solomon build an high place for Chemosh, the abomination of Moab, in the hill that is before Jerusalem, and for Molech, the abomination of the children of Ammon. And likewise did he for all his strange wives, which burnt incense and sacrificed unto their gods. Guidance: The apostasy itemized. (1) Timing: 'when Solomon was old.' The drift came late, not early. Solomon was about 60. The young king who prayed at Gibeon for an understanding heart had become an aging monarch whose heart 'was not perfect with the LORD his God.' Hebrew lo hayah levavo shalem — his heart was not whole (compare 1 Kgs 8:61, where Solomon himself had charged the people, 'Let your heart therefore be perfect with the LORD our God'). He failed his own standard. (2) The comparison: 'as was the heart of David his father.' David's heart had its specific failings — Bathsheba, the census — but it remained whole-hearted toward the LORD. He never turned to other gods. Solomon now does. The chapter is showing us the precise diagnostic difference between father and son: not moral perfection, but covenant loyalty. (3) The specific gods. Four are named: Ashtoreth (the Phoenician fertility goddess of Sidon, the consort of Baal); Milcom (the chief god of the Ammonites, an alternative spelling of Molech); Chemosh (the chief god of Moab, attested also in the Moabite Stone); and Molech (the Ammonite god whose worship involved child sacrifice — see Lev 18:21 and 2 Kgs 23:10). Each is labeled with the moral judgment: 'abomination' (shiqquts) — the standard biblical term for what causes ritual horror. (4) The locations. Solomon built 'a high place for Chemosh… in the hill that is before Jerusalem.' This is the Mount of Olives, directly across the Kidron from the Temple Mount. The deuteronomic king built pagan shrines visible from the LORD's own temple. The same hill that David ascended in tears during Absalom's rebellion (2 Sam 15:30) — and from which Jesus would later weep over Jerusalem (Luke 19:41) — became, under Solomon, the seat of foreign gods, in plain sight of the holy house. (5) The pattern: Solomon built shrines for every foreign wife's god. The harem politics of his thousand wives created a constellation of foreign cult-sites around Jerusalem. The chapter is recording the catastrophic completeness of the drift. The same hill on which the Davidic temple had been planned now hosted the chief Moabite god, the chief Ammonite god, and the child-sacrifice god of Ammon. Solomon's interfaith hospitality had crossed into outright apostasy.

And the LORD was angry with Solomon, because his heart was turned from the LORD God of Israel, which had appeared unto him twice, And had commanded him concerning this thing, that he should not go after other gods: but he kept not that which the LORD commanded. Wherefore the LORD said unto Solomon, Forasmuch as this is done of thee, and thou hast not kept my covenant and my statutes, which I have commanded thee, I will surely rend the kingdom from thee, and will give it to thy servant. Notwithstanding in thy days I will not do it for David thy father's sake: but I will rend it out of the hand of thy son. Howbeit I will not rend away all the kingdom; but will give one tribe to thy son for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake which I have chosen. Guidance: The LORD's verdict. The chapter records divine anger explicitly. The aggravating factor: 'which had appeared unto him twice' — the Gibeon dream and the post-dedication theophany. Solomon had not failed in ignorance; he had failed in the face of two direct divine encounters. The LORD had specifically commanded him 'concerning this thing, that he should not go after other gods.' The very point on which Solomon failed had been the precise content of God's prior warning. The judicial response is four-fold. (1) 'I will surely rend the kingdom from thee.' Hebrew qarea eqra — I will utterly tear. The verb qara is repeated for emphasis. The kingdom that the LORD established under David and ratified under Solomon will now be torn apart. (2) 'I will give it to thy servant.' Not to a foreign conqueror, but to one of Solomon's own subjects — a humiliation. (3) Mercy in timing: 'In thy days I will not do it for David thy father's sake.' The covenant with David provides cover for Solomon personally — the division will happen after his death, not during it. The chapter is showing us divine mercy operating even within judgment: David's life of faithfulness purchases a delayed sentence for his unfaithful son. (4) Mercy in scope: 'I will not rend away all the kingdom; but will give one tribe to thy son for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake.' One tribe (Judah, plus Benjamin and the southern remnant) will remain with the Davidic line. The eternal Davidic promise is preserved even as the immediate kingdom is fractured. The chapter is teaching us a fundamental theological truth: God's promises do not fail, but their application contracts when their recipients fail. The Davidic dynasty would continue from a much smaller territorial base — a constant reminder that the line was preserved by grace, not by Solomon's faithfulness. The eternal kingdom of the messianic Son of David would ultimately rise not from Solomonic glory but from this contracted Judean remnant.

And the LORD was angry with Solomon, because his heart was turned from the LORD God of Israel, which had appeared unto him twice, And had commanded him concerning this thing, that he should not go after other gods: but he kept not that which the LORD commanded. Wherefore the LORD said unto Solomon, Forasmuch as this is done of thee, and thou hast not kept my covenant and my statutes, which I have commanded thee, I will surely rend the kingdom from thee, and will give it to thy servant. Notwithstanding in thy days I will not do it for David thy father's sake: but I will rend it out of the hand of thy son. Howbeit I will not rend away all the kingdom; but will give one tribe to thy son for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake which I have chosen. Guidance: The LORD's verdict. The chapter records divine anger explicitly. The aggravating factor: 'which had appeared unto him twice' — the Gibeon dream and the post-dedication theophany. Solomon had not failed in ignorance; he had failed in the face of two direct divine encounters. The LORD had specifically commanded him 'concerning this thing, that he should not go after other gods.' The very point on which Solomon failed had been the precise content of God's prior warning. The judicial response is four-fold. (1) 'I will surely rend the kingdom from thee.' Hebrew qarea eqra — I will utterly tear. The verb qara is repeated for emphasis. The kingdom that the LORD established under David and ratified under Solomon will now be torn apart. (2) 'I will give it to thy servant.' Not to a foreign conqueror, but to one of Solomon's own subjects — a humiliation. (3) Mercy in timing: 'In thy days I will not do it for David thy father's sake.' The covenant with David provides cover for Solomon personally — the division will happen after his death, not during it. The chapter is showing us divine mercy operating even within judgment: David's life of faithfulness purchases a delayed sentence for his unfaithful son. (4) Mercy in scope: 'I will not rend away all the kingdom; but will give one tribe to thy son for David my servant's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake.' One tribe (Judah, plus Benjamin and the southern remnant) will remain with the Davidic line. The eternal Davidic promise is preserved even as the immediate kingdom is fractured. The chapter is teaching us a fundamental theological truth: God's promises do not fail, but their application contracts when their recipients fail. The Davidic dynasty would continue from a much smaller territorial base — a constant reminder that the line was preserved by grace, not by Solomon's faithfulness. The eternal kingdom of the messianic Son of David would ultimately rise not from Solomonic glory but from this contracted Judean remnant.

And the LORD stirred up an adversary unto Solomon, Hadad the Edomite: he was of the king's seed in Edom. For it came to pass, when David was in Edom, and Joab the captain of the host was gone up to bury the slain, after he had smitten every male in Edom; (For six months did Joab remain there with all Israel, until he had cut off every male in Edom:) That Hadad fled, he and certain Edomites of his father's servants with him, to go into Egypt; Hadad being yet a little child. And they arose out of Midian, and came to Paran: and they took men with them out of Paran, and they came to Egypt, unto Pharaoh king of Egypt; which gave him an house, and appointed him victuals, and gave him land. And Hadad found great favour in the sight of Pharaoh, so that he gave him to wife the sister of his own wife, the sister of Tahpenes the queen. And the sister of Tahpenes bare him Genubath his son, whom Tahpenes weaned in Pharaoh's house: and Genubath was in Pharaoh's household among the sons of Pharaoh. And when Hadad heard in Egypt that David slept with his fathers, and that Joab the captain of the host was dead, Hadad said to Pharaoh, Let me depart, that I may go to mine own country. Then Pharaoh said unto him, But what hast thou lacked with me, that, behold, thou seekest to go to thine own country? And he answered, Nothing: howbeit let me go in any wise. Guidance: The first adversary — Hadad of Edom. The chapter introduces three foreign and domestic enemies the LORD raises up against Solomon. Hadad's story is told in detail because it stretches back to David's reign. (1) Origin. Hadad was 'of the king's seed in Edom' — Edomite royalty. (2) The David-era catastrophe. During David's conquest of Edom (2 Sam 8:13-14; Ps 60 superscription), Joab and the Israelite army occupied the country for six months and 'cut off every male in Edom.' The Hebrew phrase is chilling — kol-zakhar — every male. This was a campaign of military extermination intended to eliminate Edomite resistance permanently. (3) Hadad's escape. The infant prince Hadad was smuggled out by surviving Edomite courtiers, who fled across the desert via Midian and Paran to Egypt. (4) Pharaoh's hospitality. The Egyptian Pharaoh (probably a Twenty-first Dynasty pharaoh, possibly Siamun) received the exiled prince, gave him a house, food, and land, and eventually married him to the queen Tahpenes' sister. Hadad became Pharaoh's brother-in-law. His son Genubath was raised in the royal nursery alongside Pharaoh's own sons. (5) The return. When Hadad heard that both David and Joab (the architect of Edom's destruction) had died, he asked Pharaoh's permission to return home. Pharaoh, surprised — 'What hast thou lacked with me?' — eventually let him go. The chapter is showing us a long-term divine providence working through ordinary political channels. Forty or fifty years before Solomon's apostasy, the LORD had preserved an Edomite prince in Egypt. Now, in the LORD's perfect timing, Hadad emerges as Solomon's adversary. The chapter is teaching us that the consequences of unfaithful kingship are not random misfortunes but the LORD's deliberate raising-up of opposition. The Edomite hostility Solomon now faces was sown in his father's day and is being harvested in his own — a sober reminder that the LORD's discipline often uses long-standing geopolitical realities to bring sin home to a king's door.

The Hebrew phrase is chilling — kol-zakhar — every male. This was a campaign of military extermination intended to eliminate Edomite resistance permanently. (3) Hadad's escape. The infant prince Hadad was smuggled out by surviving Edomite courtiers, who fled across the desert via Midian and Paran to Egypt. (4) Pharaoh's hospitality. The Egyptian Pharaoh (probably a Twenty-first Dynasty pharaoh, possibly Siamun) received the exiled prince, gave him a house, food, and land, and eventually married him to the queen Tahpenes' sister. Hadad became Pharaoh's brother-in-law. His son Genubath was raised in the royal nursery alongside Pharaoh's own sons. (5) The return. When Hadad heard that both David and Joab (the architect of Edom's destruction) had died, he asked Pharaoh's permission to return home. Pharaoh, surprised — 'What hast thou lacked with me?' — eventually let him go. The chapter is showing us a long-term divine providence working through ordinary political channels. Forty or fifty years before Solomon's apostasy, the LORD had preserved an Edomite prince in Egypt. Now, in the LORD's perfect timing, Hadad emerges as Solomon's adversary. The chapter is teaching us that the consequences of unfaithful kingship are not random misfortunes but the LORD's deliberate raising-up of opposition. The Edomite hostility Solomon now faces was sown in his father's day and is being harvested in his own — a sober reminder that the LORD's discipline often uses long-standing geopolitical realities to bring sin home to a king's door.

And God stirred him up another adversary, Rezon the son of Eliadah, which fled from his lord Hadadezer king of Zobah: And he gathered men unto him, and became captain over a band, when David slew them of Zobah: and they went to Damascus, and dwelt therein, and reigned in Damascus. And he was an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon, beside the mischief that Hadad did: and he abhorred Israel, and reigned over Syria. Guidance: The second adversary — Rezon of Aram. His backstory also goes back to David. Rezon had been a servant of Hadadezer, the Aramean king of Zobah whom David had defeated in 2 Sam 8:3-8. During the catastrophe, Rezon fled his master, gathered a band of military exiles around himself, and over the years built it into a force. They eventually seized Damascus and made it their capital. Rezon set himself up as the founding king of the Aramean kingdom of Damascus — the same Damascus that would harass Israel for the next two and a half centuries. 'He was an adversary to Israel all the days of Solomon… and he abhorred Israel.' The Hebrew vayyaqotz — 'he loathed' — captures Rezon's enduring hostility. The chapter is recording the rise of one of the great regional threats to Israelite stability. Where Hadad disrupted Solomon's southern flank, Rezon disrupted the northern. The Solomonic empire that had stretched 'from the river [Euphrates] unto the land of the Philistines' (1 Kgs 4:21) was being squeezed inward from both ends. The chapter is teaching us a sober principle: the LORD's disciplinary instruments are often not new creations but existing political enemies whose moment has come. Rezon had been building his power for decades; the LORD's timing released him to disrupt Israel precisely when Israel had given the LORD reason to send disruption.

And Jeroboam the son of Nebat, an Ephrathite of Zereda, Solomon's servant, whose mother's name was Zeruah, a widow woman, even he lifted up his hand against the king. And this was the cause that he lifted up his hand against the king: Solomon built Millo, and repaired the breaches of the city of David his father. And the man Jeroboam was a mighty man of valour: and Solomon seeing the young man that he was industrious, he made him ruler over all the charge of the house of Joseph. Guidance: The third adversary — Jeroboam — different from the others because he is an Israelite. Jeroboam is introduced with care. He was 'an Ephrathite of Zereda' — from the central tribe of Ephraim, son of a widow named Zeruah. His father Nebat had died young. He was 'Solomon's servant' — meaning a royal officer or court official. The pivotal characterization: 'a mighty man of valour' (gibbor chayil — the same phrase used for David's elite warriors). Solomon, recognizing his industriousness, promoted him to 'ruler over all the charge of the house of Joseph' — superintendent of the corvée labor from the northern tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh, the descendants of Joseph). The chapter is showing us a tragic irony: Solomon himself elevated the man who would tear the kingdom from his son. Jeroboam was Solomon's trusted northern administrator. The very efficiency that made him useful made him dangerous. The northern tribes resented the heavy corvée burden Solomon's projects required; Jeroboam supervised that burden; the northerners knew him; if a revolt were ever to coalesce against the Davidic dynasty, Jeroboam was perfectly positioned to lead it. The chapter is teaching us a leadership principle: the qualities that produce competent subordinates also produce capable rivals. A king who promotes ambitious, talented men gains good administration but cultivates potential challengers. Solomon's lavish building program demanded such administrators; the moral price of the building program is here named — it produced both the discontent of the laborers and the leader who could channel that discontent.

And it came to pass at that time when Jeroboam went out of Jerusalem, that the prophet Ahijah the Shilonite found him in the way; and he had clad himself with a new garment; and they two were alone in the field: And Ahijah caught the new garment that was on him, and rent it in twelve pieces: And he said to Jeroboam, Take thee ten pieces: for thus saith the LORD, the God of Israel, Behold, I will rend the kingdom out of the hand of Solomon, and will give ten tribes to thee: (But he shall have one tribe for my servant David's sake, and for Jerusalem's sake, the city which I have chosen out of all the tribes of Israel:) Because that they have forsaken me, and have worshipped Ashtoreth the goddess of the Zidonians, Chemosh the god of the Moabites, and Milcom the god of the children of Ammon, and have not walked in my ways, to do that which is right in mine eyes, and to keep my statutes and my judgments, as did David his father. Howbeit I will not take the whole kingdom out of his hand: but I will make him prince all the days of his life for David my servant's sake, whom I chose, because he kept my commandments and my statutes: But I will take the kingdom out of his son's hand, and will give it unto thee, even ten tribes. And unto his son will I give one tribe, that David my servant may have a light alway before me in Jerusalem, the city which I have chosen me to put my name there. And I will take thee, and thou shalt reign according to all that thy soul desireth, and shalt be king over Israel. And it shall be, if thou wilt hearken unto all that I command thee, and wilt walk in my ways, and do that is right in my sight, to keep my statutes and my commandments, as David my servant did; that I will be with thee, and build thee a sure house, as I built for David, and will give Israel unto thee. And I will for this afflict the seed of David, but not for ever. Guidance: Ahijah's torn-cloak prophecy — one of the most dramatic prophetic acts in 1 Kings. Ahijah was a prophet from Shiloh (the ancient pre-Davidic central sanctuary). He intercepts Jeroboam alone in a field outside Jerusalem, removes his own new cloak, and tears it deliberately into twelve pieces. Then he hands Jeroboam ten of the pieces with the verbal interpretation: 'Take thee ten pieces: for thus saith the LORD, the God of Israel, Behold, I will rend the kingdom out of the hand of Solomon, and will give ten tribes to thee.' The torn-cloak is a prophetic sign-act in the tradition of Samuel tearing Saul's robe (1 Sam 15:27-28). The visual is unmistakable: the kingdom of Israel is being torn into pieces. Twelve pieces for twelve tribes. Ten given to Jeroboam; two remaining (Judah and Benjamin) — though Ahijah's speech-version says one, perhaps treating Benjamin as included with Judah or held closely enough not to be counted separately. The terms. (1) The reason for the rending: 'they have forsaken me, and have worshipped Ashtoreth, Chemosh, Milcom… and have not walked in my ways.' Apostasy — Solomon's, but the indictment is plural: 'they have forsaken me.' The whole nation has drifted, not just the king. (2) Mercy reserved: 'I will not take the whole kingdom out of his hand: but I will make him prince all the days of his life for David my servant's sake.' Solomon retains the kingdom; the division comes after his death. (3) Mercy for the Davidic line: 'unto his son will I give one tribe, that David my servant may have a light alway before me in Jerusalem.' The Hebrew nir — lamp — recalls 2 Sam 21:17, where David's men called him 'the lamp of Israel.' The Davidic lamp will continue burning, however small the flame, in Jerusalem. (4) The promise to Jeroboam: 'If thou wilt hearken unto all that I command thee, and wilt walk in my ways… I will build thee a sure house, as I built for David.' Jeroboam is offered the same dynastic stability that the LORD had granted David — conditionally. If Jeroboam walks faithfully, his line will be permanent. (5) The closing assurance: the affliction of David's seed is not forever. The Davidic line will be reduced but not extinguished. The chapter is showing us divine sovereignty at work through ordinary political opposition. The LORD has chosen Jeroboam; the LORD will give him ten tribes; the LORD has set the timing for after Solomon's death; the LORD has reserved a Davidic remnant. Jeroboam, hearing this, knows the throne is his — but conditionally. The historical narrator will record in coming chapters whether Jeroboam keeps the condition or not.

Solomon sought therefore to kill Jeroboam. And Jeroboam arose, and fled into Egypt, unto Shishak king of Egypt, and was in Egypt until the death of Solomon. Guidance: Solomon's attempted murder of his servant. Word of Ahijah's prophecy must have reached the king's ears — perhaps through Solomon's intelligence network. Solomon, recognizing the threat, tries to eliminate Jeroboam. The verb va-yevaqqesh — 'and he sought' — indicates active hunting. Jeroboam flees to Egypt — specifically to Shishak (the Twenty-second Dynasty Pharaoh, the same Pharaoh who would invade Judah under Rehoboam — 1 Kgs 14:25-26). Egypt has become the standard refuge for those fleeing Israelite kings — Hadad fled to Egypt during David's reign; now Jeroboam flees to Egypt during Solomon's. The chapter is showing us the parallel: just as Hadad had been sheltered to grow into Solomon's adversary, so Jeroboam now is sheltered to become Solomon's successor-in-rebellion. The same Pharaoh hosts both. The chapter is recording a remarkable irony: Solomon, who had married Pharaoh's daughter to seal an alliance with Egypt, finds Egypt sheltering the man the LORD has chosen to tear away ten tribes of his kingdom. Foreign-marriage diplomacy has not bought him the loyalty he hoped for. The wise king's wisdom does not save him from his own apostasy's consequences. Jeroboam waits out Solomon's reign in Egypt — patiently, with Egyptian backing, awaiting his moment to return. The moment will come in chapter 12.

And the rest of the acts of Solomon, and all that he did, and his wisdom, are they not written in the book of the acts of Solomon? And the time that Solomon reigned in Jerusalem over all Israel was forty years. And Solomon slept with his fathers, and was buried in the city of David his father: and Rehoboam his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: The chapter — and the long Solomonic narrative — closes with the formal regnal summary. (1) The literary source-note: 'the book of the acts of Solomon' (sepher divrei Shelomo) is referenced as a fuller annal from which the canonical narrator drew his selection. This book is now lost; the canonical 1 Kings preserves only what the narrator judged theologically essential. (2) The reign length: forty years. The same as David's, the same as the wilderness wandering — the standard biblical generation. Most modern chronologies place his reign roughly 970-930 BC. (3) The succession: 'Solomon slept with his fathers, and was buried in the city of David his father.' The formula of dignified royal death. He died in his early sixties — short of the long-life the LORD had conditionally promised at Gibeon if he kept covenant (1 Kgs 3:14). The conditional blessing was not fully received. (4) Rehoboam succeeds. The son of the Ammonite princess Naamah (1 Kgs 14:21) — already, the foreign-marriage problem has produced the next generation's king. The chapter is closing the great Solomonic arc with quiet sobriety. The king who began in dazzling promise at Gibeon ends in apostasy, hunted political rivals, and a kingdom marked for division. His son will preside over the actual fragmentation in chapter 12. The chapter is teaching us a final lesson: God's blessings, however great, are not self-sustaining. They depend on continuing fidelity. The temple Solomon built will stand 380 years; the empire Solomon assembled will dissolve within months of his death. The wisdom Solomon embodied will be preserved in his Proverbs but will not save his own family from division. The chapter ends with the readers' eyes turned forward — to Rehoboam's failure (ch 12), to the divided monarchy's long descent (chs 13-22 and beyond), and ultimately to the eternal Davidic king (the Messiah) who alone will perfectly fulfill the conditions Solomon could not keep. The historical Solomon recedes; the theological lesson he embodies — the impossibility of human royal perfection — remains.
